[2026-07-04 22:57:21 INFO] Process design reviewer: **Process design reviewer<->Team leader on : GiveSuggestion, turn 0**

[You're a team leader. Now, we share a common interest in collaborating to successfully complete a task assigned by a new customer.
Your main responsibilities will include guiding the process design expert through process generation, process refinement, and so on.
Here is a new customer's task: Module 2: Early Treatment
This module identifies best practices for the ED treatment of TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke. The
majority of TIA patients and some patients with minor stroke do not require admission to hospital
and should be referred to an urgent TIA/minor stroke unit/TIA clinic/stroke-prevention clinic or
comparable ambulatory care setting for rapid diagnostic and medical evaluation. All patients should
be given appropriate cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies (Modules 5 and 10).
Module 2 Recommended Practices 
2.1 Glucose management
2.1.1 Hypoglycemia should be corrected immediately
2.1.2 Blood glucose measurement should be repeated if the first
random glucose value is > 11 mmol/L
2.2 Body temperature
2.2.1 Temperature should be evaluated as part of routine vital signs
every 4 hours for first 48 hours
For temperature > 37.5 C:
• initiate temperature-reducing measures
• investigate potential infection
• initiate antipyretic and antimicrobial therapy as required
2.3 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies
2.3.1 All patients, whether admitted to hospital or discharged, should
be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary prevention
assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10)
2.4 Management of patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
2.4.1 The following OHTAC recommendations should be followed:
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA with high-
risk featuresa or a minor strokeb undergo a brain CT scan and
initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated)
as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset,
followed by other stroke-prevention treatments tailored to each
patient. With respect to the location of care, OHTAC recommends
that:
o such immediate care be provided at a specialized TIA/minor
stroke clinicc
o where delays to accessing a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic
pose risks to patient health, evaluation (as outlined above) occur
at an appropriately resourced ED, and further consideration be
given to inpatient evaluation and management for stroke
prevention. OHTAC further recommends the establishment of
accreditation standards for TIA/minor stroke care to ensure
equitable access to appropriate, high-quality care, irrespective of
the location of initial presentation
o where medical attention has been sought after 48 hours from
symptom onset, patients be referred for evaluation at a
specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic or alternatively an outpatient
clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd within 24 hours of initial
presentation
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA without
high-risk featuresa undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of
antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as
possible and no later than 24 hours after initial presentation,
followed by referral to an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention
servicesd for comprehensive evaluation and management within 1
month of symptom onset
2.4.2 Some TIA patients, such as those who present with fluctuating or
crescendo hemibodye motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech
difficulty, should be evaluated in the ED to be considered for admission
to hospital
2.4.3 Patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke who are not on
an antiplatelet agent at the time of presentation should be started on
antiplatelet therapy immediately with 1 of the following after brain
imaging has excluded intracranial hemorrhage:
• ASA 160 mg loading dose, followed by enteric coated ASA (81–
325 mg) daily. Most patients should be on a maintenance dose of
81 mg/day
• clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose, followed by 75 mg/day
• extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg/ASA 25 mg bid (load with
ASA 160 mg first)
2.4.4 Rectal or gastric ASA should be offered as necessary, such as
for patients who fail swallowing screening
2.5–2.8 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; Australia, Australian Clinical analysis; ED, emergency department; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; OHTAC, Ontario Health Technology Advisory Committee; NHS/NICE,
National Collaborating Centre for Chronic Conditions; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack.
aSymptoms consistent with a hemispheric event, including sudden hemiparesis, speech difficulties, or monocular vision loss, and/or known high
risk conditions associated with stroke, including atrial fibrillation (especially if inadequately anticoagulated) or known carotid artery
atherosclerosis with > 50% stenosis (narrowing) on the side consistent with the hemispheric event.
bPatients with very mild persistent symptoms or no residual symptoms but with a small asymptomatic infarct (stroke) on imaging
cA clinic with stroke expertise and the resources to conduct all necessary investigations in 1 place to initiate rapid treatment, including brain and
vascular imaging, heart monitoring, and laboratory tests.
dFor example, a provincial stroke-prevention clinic or community neurology/internal medicine clinic with a stroke-prevention focus.
eMotor weakness may be in 1 body segment (face, arm, or leg), but sensory loss must involve at least 2 contiguous segments (face/arm or
arm/leg) on 1 side of the body to be considered highest risk.

The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel.
Module 2 Implementation Considerations
General considerations
• Standardized priority protocols should be established for all suspected stroke patients to receive treatment as soon as
possible and be admitted to a stroke unit within the first few hours after presenting to an ED
• Hospitals should have ready access to rectal ASA
Abbreviations: ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; ED, emergency department..
To complete the task, I will give you one or more instructions, and you must help me to write a specific solution that appropriately solves the requested instruction based on your expertise and my needs.]

Problem: In the high-risk/minor stroke pathway, the branch with id='branch16' under the exclusive gateway with id='exclusivegateway5' is logically incorrect because activity id='activity14' (“refer for evaluation within 24 hours of initial presentation”) is followed by the common activities id='activity19' to id='activity30', which means patients who seek medical attention more than 48 hours after symptom onset are still forced into the same immediate urgent CT/antiplatelet evaluation block as the within-48-hour patients.

Suggested modification: Adjust the scope and order of the branches under id='exclusivegateway5'.
- Keep id='activity14' inside id='branch16' as the main action for the “more than 48 hours after symptom onset” branch.
- Remove the common placement of id='activity19', id='activity20_1', id='activity20_2', id='activity20_3', id='exclusivegateway7', and id='activity30' after id='exclusivegateway5'.
- Place the urgent CT and antiplatelet initiation sequence only in the branch for patients presenting within 48 hours after symptom onset, or, if you want to model the >48-hour pathway in detail, create a separate evaluation sequence inside id='branch16' with the referred service as the role and timing changed to “within 24 hours of initial presentation,” not “no later than 24 hours after symptom onset.”

Reason: The recommendation clearly separates these two situations. For patients presenting within 48 hours, urgent CT and antiplatelet treatment should occur as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset. For patients presenting more than 48 hours after symptom onset, the guideline says they should be referred for evaluation within 24 hours of initial presentation. Therefore, the current design makes the exclusive gateway ineffective and mixes two different clinical timing rules into one downstream sequence.

[2026-07-04 22:57:21 INFO] flask app.py did not start for online log
[2026-07-04 22:57:21 INFO] **[Seminar Conclusion]**:

 Problem: In the high-risk/minor stroke pathway, the branch with id='branch16' under the exclusive gateway with id='exclusivegateway5' is logically incorrect because activity id='activity14' (“refer for evaluation within 24 hours of initial presentation”) is followed by the common activities id='activity19' to id='activity30', which means patients who seek medical attention more than 48 hours after symptom onset are still forced into the same immediate urgent CT/antiplatelet evaluation block as the within-48-hour patients.

Suggested modification: Adjust the scope and order of the branches under id='exclusivegateway5'.
- Keep id='activity14' inside id='branch16' as the main action for the “more than 48 hours after symptom onset” branch.
- Remove the common placement of id='activity19', id='activity20_1', id='activity20_2', id='activity20_3', id='exclusivegateway7', and id='activity30' after id='exclusivegateway5'.
- Place the urgent CT and antiplatelet initiation sequence only in the branch for patients presenting within 48 hours after symptom onset, or, if you want to model the >48-hour pathway in detail, create a separate evaluation sequence inside id='branch16' with the referred service as the role and timing changed to “within 24 hours of initial presentation,” not “no later than 24 hours after symptom onset.”

Reason: The recommendation clearly separates these two situations. For patients presenting within 48 hours, urgent CT and antiplatelet treatment should occur as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset. For patients presenting more than 48 hours after symptom onset, the guideline says they should be referred for evaluation within 24 hours of initial presentation. Therefore, the current design makes the exclusive gateway ineffective and mixes two different clinical timing rules into one downstream sequence.

| **task_prompt** | Module 2: Early Treatment This module identifies best practices for the ED treatment of TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke. The majority of TIA patients and some patients with minor stroke do not require admission to hospital and should be referred to an urgent TIA/minor stroke unit/TIA clinic/stroke-prevention clinic or comparable ambulatory care setting for rapid diagnostic and medical evaluation. All patients should be given appropriate cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies (Modules 5 and 10). Module 2 Recommended Practices  2.1 Glucose management 2.1.1 Hypoglycemia should be corrected immediately 2.1.2 Blood glucose measurement should be repeated if the first random glucose value is &gt; 11 mmol/L 2.2 Body temperature 2.2.1 Temperature should be evaluated as part of routine vital signs every 4 hours for first 48 hours For temperature &gt; 37.5 C: • initiate temperature-reducing measures • investigate potential infection • initiate antipyretic and antimicrobial therapy as required 2.3 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies 2.3.1 All patients, whether admitted to hospital or discharged, should be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary prevention assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10) 2.4 Management of patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke 2.4.1 The following OHTAC recommendations should be followed: • OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA with high- risk featuresa or a minor strokeb undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset, followed by other stroke-prevention treatments tailored to each patient. With respect to the location of care, OHTAC recommends that: o such immediate care be provided at a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinicc o where delays to accessing a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic pose risks to patient health, evaluation (as outlined above) occur at an appropriately resourced ED, and further consideration be given to inpatient evaluation and management for stroke prevention. OHTAC further recommends the establishment of accreditation standards for TIA/minor stroke care to ensure equitable access to appropriate, high-quality care, irrespective of the location of initial presentation o where medical attention has been sought after 48 hours from symptom onset, patients be referred for evaluation at a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic or alternatively an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd within 24 hours of initial presentation • OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA without high-risk featuresa undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after initial presentation, followed by referral to an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd for comprehensive evaluation and management within 1 month of symptom onset 2.4.2 Some TIA patients, such as those who present with fluctuating or crescendo hemibodye motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech difficulty, should be evaluated in the ED to be considered for admission to hospital 2.4.3 Patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke who are not on an antiplatelet agent at the time of presentation should be started on antiplatelet therapy immediately with 1 of the following after brain imaging has excluded intracranial hemorrhage: • ASA 160 mg loading dose, followed by enteric coated ASA (81– 325 mg) daily. Most patients should be on a maintenance dose of 81 mg/day • clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose, followed by 75 mg/day • extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg/ASA 25 mg bid (load with ASA 160 mg first) 2.4.4 Rectal or gastric ASA should be offered as necessary, such as for patients who fail swallowing screening 2.5–2.8 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; Australia, Australian Clinical analysis; ED, emergency department; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; OHTAC, Ontario Health Technology Advisory Committee; NHS/NICE, National Collaborating Centre for Chronic Conditions; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack. aSymptoms consistent with a hemispheric event, including sudden hemiparesis, speech difficulties, or monocular vision loss, and/or known high risk conditions associated with stroke, including atrial fibrillation (especially if inadequately anticoagulated) or known carotid artery atherosclerosis with &gt; 50% stenosis (narrowing) on the side consistent with the hemispheric event. bPatients with very mild persistent symptoms or no residual symptoms but with a small asymptomatic infarct (stroke) on imaging cA clinic with stroke expertise and the resources to conduct all necessary investigations in 1 place to initiate rapid treatment, including brain and vascular imaging, heart monitoring, and laboratory tests. dFor example, a provincial stroke-prevention clinic or community neurology/internal medicine clinic with a stroke-prevention focus. eMotor weakness may be in 1 body segment (face, arm, or leg), but sensory loss must involve at least 2 contiguous segments (face/arm or arm/leg) on 1 side of the body to be considered highest risk. The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel. For patients presenting within 48 hours, urgent CT and antiplatelet treatment should occur as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset. For patients presenting more than 48 hours after symptom onset, the guideline says they should be referred for evaluation within 24 hours of initial presentation. Therefore, the current design makes the exclusive gateway ineffective and mixes two different clinical timing rules into one downstream sequence.", 'format': "\nWhen the process starts, please output ''\nAn activity is represented as follows:\n\nThe exclusive gateway is represented as follows:\n\n  \n    \n  \n  \n    \n  \n\nThe parallel gateway is represented as follows:\n\n  \n    \n  \n  \n    \n  \n\nThe inclusive gateway is represented as follows:\n\n  \n    \n  \n  \n    \n  \n\nAt the end of the process, please output ''\n\nThere are a few requirements that should be noted when generating process text:\n    1) role, action, objects and id in an activity represent the subject of the activity, the activity behavior, the items needed to perform in the activity and the activity id, respectively.\n    2) There are at least two branches within the exclusive gateway and the parallel gateway.\n    3) Activity, gateway, and branch id attributes must be unique.\n    4) When the branch in the exclusive gateway has a condition, fill the condition into the condition attribute. When the branch unconditional within the exclusive gateway, the condition attribute of the branch =''. However, all branches within a exclusive gateway can only be conditional, or both unconditional.\n    5) The branch in the parallel gateway has no condition attribute.\n    6) Each branch in the inclusive gateway must have condition.\n    7) There must be at least one activity inside the branch tag.\n    8) The activity of a sequential relation need not be represented by a gateway, that is, there is no 'sequenceGateway' label.\n", 'examples': ' '} |

[2026-07-04 22:57:21 INFO] Process design reviewer: **[Start Chat]**

[You're a process design expert. Now, we share a common interest in collaborating to successfully complete a task assigned by a new customer.
You are familiar with exclusive gateway, parallel gateway and inclusive gateway in BPMN. Your main responsibilities include process generation, process refinement, process review and so on, guided by team leader.
You model the process of a clinical practice guideline to a clinical pathway.
Here is a new customer's task: Module 2: Early Treatment
This module identifies best practices for the ED treatment of TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke. The
majority of TIA patients and some patients with minor stroke do not require admission to hospital
and should be referred to an urgent TIA/minor stroke unit/TIA clinic/stroke-prevention clinic or
comparable ambulatory care setting for rapid diagnostic and medical evaluation. All patients should
be given appropriate cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies (Modules 5 and 10).
Module 2 Recommended Practices 
2.1 Glucose management
2.1.1 Hypoglycemia should be corrected immediately
2.1.2 Blood glucose measurement should be repeated if the first
random glucose value is > 11 mmol/L
2.2 Body temperature
2.2.1 Temperature should be evaluated as part of routine vital signs
every 4 hours for first 48 hours
For temperature > 37.5 C:
• initiate temperature-reducing measures
• investigate potential infection
• initiate antipyretic and antimicrobial therapy as required
2.3 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies
2.3.1 All patients, whether admitted to hospital or discharged, should
be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary prevention
assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10)
2.4 Management of patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
2.4.1 The following OHTAC recommendations should be followed:
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA with high-
risk featuresa or a minor strokeb undergo a brain CT scan and
initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated)
as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset,
followed by other stroke-prevention treatments tailored to each
patient. With respect to the location of care, OHTAC recommends
that:
o such immediate care be provided at a specialized TIA/minor
stroke clinicc
o where delays to accessing a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic
pose risks to patient health, evaluation (as outlined above) occur
at an appropriately resourced ED, and further consideration be
given to inpatient evaluation and management for stroke
prevention. OHTAC further recommends the establishment of
accreditation standards for TIA/minor stroke care to ensure
equitable access to appropriate, high-quality care, irrespective of
the location of initial presentation
o where medical attention has been sought after 48 hours from
symptom onset, patients be referred for evaluation at a
specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic or alternatively an outpatient
clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd within 24 hours of initial
presentation
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA without
high-risk featuresa undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of
antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as
possible and no later than 24 hours after initial presentation,
followed by referral to an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention
servicesd for comprehensive evaluation and management within 1
month of symptom onset
2.4.2 Some TIA patients, such as those who present with fluctuating or
crescendo hemibodye motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech
difficulty, should be evaluated in the ED to be considered for admission
to hospital
2.4.3 Patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke who are not on
an antiplatelet agent at the time of presentation should be started on
antiplatelet therapy immediately with 1 of the following after brain
imaging has excluded intracranial hemorrhage:
• ASA 160 mg loading dose, followed by enteric coated ASA (81–
325 mg) daily. Most patients should be on a maintenance dose of
81 mg/day
• clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose, followed by 75 mg/day
• extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg/ASA 25 mg bid (load with
ASA 160 mg first)
2.4.4 Rectal or gastric ASA should be offered as necessary, such as
for patients who fail swallowing screening
2.5–2.8 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; Australia, Australian Clinical analysis; ED, emergency department; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; OHTAC, Ontario Health Technology Advisory Committee; NHS/NICE,
National Collaborating Centre for Chronic Conditions; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack.
aSymptoms consistent with a hemispheric event, including sudden hemiparesis, speech difficulties, or monocular vision loss, and/or known high
risk conditions associated with stroke, including atrial fibrillation (especially if inadequately anticoagulated) or known carotid artery
atherosclerosis with > 50% stenosis (narrowing) on the side consistent with the hemispheric event.
bPatients with very mild persistent symptoms or no residual symptoms but with a small asymptomatic infarct (stroke) on imaging
cA clinic with stroke expertise and the resources to conduct all necessary investigations in 1 place to initiate rapid treatment, including brain and
vascular imaging, heart monitoring, and laboratory tests.
dFor example, a provincial stroke-prevention clinic or community neurology/internal medicine clinic with a stroke-prevention focus.
eMotor weakness may be in 1 body segment (face, arm, or leg), but sensory loss must involve at least 2 contiguous segments (face/arm or
arm/leg) on 1 side of the body to be considered highest risk.

The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel.
Module 2 Implementation Considerations
General considerations
• Standardized priority protocols should be established for all suspected stroke patients to receive treatment as soon as
possible and be admitted to a stroke unit within the first few hours after presenting to an ED
• Hospitals should have ready access to rectal ASA
Abbreviations: ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; ED, emergency department..
To complete the task, I will give you one or more instructions, and you must help me to write a specific solution that appropriately solves the requested instruction based on your expertise and my needs.]

task: Module 2: Early Treatment
This module identifies best practices for the ED treatment of TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke. The
majority of TIA patients and some patients with minor stroke do not require admission to hospital
and should be referred to an urgent TIA/minor stroke unit/TIA clinic/stroke-prevention clinic or
comparable ambulatory care setting for rapid diagnostic and medical evaluation. All patients should
be given appropriate cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies (Modules 5 and 10).
Module 2 Recommended Practices 
2.1 Glucose management
2.1.1 Hypoglycemia should be corrected immediately
2.1.2 Blood glucose measurement should be repeated if the first
random glucose value is > 11 mmol/L
2.2 Body temperature
2.2.1 Temperature should be evaluated as part of routine vital signs
every 4 hours for first 48 hours
For temperature > 37.5 C:
• initiate temperature-reducing measures
• investigate potential infection
• initiate antipyretic and antimicrobial therapy as required
2.3 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies
2.3.1 All patients, whether admitted to hospital or discharged, should
be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary prevention
assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10)
2.4 Management of patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
2.4.1 The following OHTAC recommendations should be followed:
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA with high-
risk featuresa or a minor strokeb undergo a brain CT scan and
initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated)
as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset,
followed by other stroke-prevention treatments tailored to each
patient. With respect to the location of care, OHTAC recommends
that:
o such immediate care be provided at a specialized TIA/minor
stroke clinicc
o where delays to accessing a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic
pose risks to patient health, evaluation (as outlined above) occur
at an appropriately resourced ED, and further consideration be
given to inpatient evaluation and management for stroke
prevention. OHTAC further recommends the establishment of
accreditation standards for TIA/minor stroke care to ensure
equitable access to appropriate, high-quality care, irrespective of
the location of initial presentation
o where medical attention has been sought after 48 hours from
symptom onset, patients be referred for evaluation at a
specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic or alternatively an outpatient
clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd within 24 hours of initial
presentation
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA without
high-risk featuresa undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of
antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as
possible and no later than 24 hours after initial presentation,
followed by referral to an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention
servicesd for comprehensive evaluation and management within 1
month of symptom onset
2.4.2 Some TIA patients, such as those who present with fluctuating or
crescendo hemibodye motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech
difficulty, should be evaluated in the ED to be considered for admission
to hospital
2.4.3 Patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke who are not on
an antiplatelet agent at the time of presentation should be started on
antiplatelet therapy immediately with 1 of the following after brain
imaging has excluded intracranial hemorrhage:
• ASA 160 mg loading dose, followed by enteric coated ASA (81–
325 mg) daily. Most patients should be on a maintenance dose of
81 mg/day
• clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose, followed by 75 mg/day
• extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg/ASA 25 mg bid (load with
ASA 160 mg first)
2.4.4 Rectal or gastric ASA should be offered as necessary, such as
for patients who fail swallowing screening
2.5–2.8 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; Australia, Australian Clinical analysis; ED, emergency department; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; OHTAC, Ontario Health Technology Advisory Committee; NHS/NICE,
National Collaborating Centre for Chronic Conditions; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack.
aSymptoms consistent with a hemispheric event, including sudden hemiparesis, speech difficulties, or monocular vision loss, and/or known high
risk conditions associated with stroke, including atrial fibrillation (especially if inadequately anticoagulated) or known carotid artery
atherosclerosis with > 50% stenosis (narrowing) on the side consistent with the hemispheric event.
bPatients with very mild persistent symptoms or no residual symptoms but with a small asymptomatic infarct (stroke) on imaging
cA clinic with stroke expertise and the resources to conduct all necessary investigations in 1 place to initiate rapid treatment, including brain and
vascular imaging, heart monitoring, and laboratory tests.
dFor example, a provincial stroke-prevention clinic or community neurology/internal medicine clinic with a stroke-prevention focus.
eMotor weakness may be in 1 body segment (face, arm, or leg), but sensory loss must involve at least 2 contiguous segments (face/arm or
arm/leg) on 1 side of the body to be considered highest risk.

The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel.
Module 2 Implementation Considerations
General considerations
• Standardized priority protocols should be established for all suspected stroke patients to receive treatment as soon as
possible and be admitted to a stroke unit within the first few hours after presenting to an ED
• Hospitals should have ready access to rectal ASA
Abbreviations: ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; ED, emergency department.
process text: 
<process>
  <activity role='ED nurse' action='triage patient under the standardized suspected stroke priority protocol' objects='suspected TIA or minor stroke patient' id='activity1_1'/>
  <activity role='ED team' action='notify the ED physician or stroke care team and begin the urgent treatment pathway' objects='suspected TIA or minor stroke patient' id='activity1_2'/>
  <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway1'>
    <branch id='branch1'>
      <activity role='ED nurse' action='measure random blood glucose' objects='blood glucose sample' id='activity2'/>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway1'>
        <branch condition='hypoglycemia is present' id='branch4'>
          <activity role='ED team' action='correct immediately' objects='hypoglycemia' id='activity3'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='hypoglycemia is not present' id='branch5'>
          <activity role='ED team' action='continue glucose management without immediate hypoglycemia correction' objects='blood glucose result' id='activity4'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway2'>
        <branch condition='first random glucose value > 11 mmol/L' id='branch6'>
          <activity role='ED nurse' action='repeat blood glucose measurement' objects='repeat blood glucose sample' id='activity5'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='first random glucose value <= 11 mmol/L' id='branch7'>
          <activity role='ED nurse' action='document that repeat blood glucose measurement is not required' objects='initial blood glucose result' id='activity6'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch2'>
      <activity role='ED nurse' action='evaluate temperature as part of routine vital signs every 4 hours for the first 48 hours' objects='temperature and vital signs' id='activity7'/>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway3'>
        <branch condition='temperature > 37.5 C' id='branch8'>
          <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway2'>
            <branch id='branch10'>
              <activity role='ED team' action='initiate temperature-reducing measures' objects='elevated body temperature' id='activity8'/>
            </branch>
            <branch id='branch11'>
              <activity role='ED team' action='investigate potential infection' objects='possible infection source' id='activity9'/>
            </branch>
          </parallelGateway>
          <inclusiveGateway id='inclusivegateway1'>
            <branch condition='antipyretic therapy is required' id='branch12'>
              <activity role='ED physician' action='initiate antipyretic therapy' objects='antipyretic medication' id='activity10'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='antimicrobial therapy is required' id='branch13'>
              <activity role='ED physician' action='initiate antimicrobial therapy' objects='antimicrobial medication' id='activity11'/>
            </branch>
          </inclusiveGateway>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='temperature <= 37.5 C' id='branch9'>
          <activity role='ED nurse' action='continue routine temperature monitoring every 4 hours for the first 48 hours' objects='temperature observations' id='activity12'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch3'>
      <activity role='ED physician' action='assess neurologic symptoms and whether deficits are nondisabling or persistent' objects='neurologic symptoms and current deficits' id='activity13_1'/>
      <activity role='ED physician' action='determine time of symptom onset and time of first medical attention' objects='onset history and presentation timing' id='activity13_2'/>
      <activity role='ED physician' action='identify high-risk TIA features and fluctuating or crescendo symptoms' objects='risk profile and admission warning features' id='activity13_3'/>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway4'>
        <branch condition='TIA with high-risk features or minor nondisabling stroke' id='branch14'>
          <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway5'>
            <branch condition='medical attention is sought more than 48 hours after symptom onset' id='branch16'>
              <activity role='ED physician' action='refer for evaluation within 24 hours of initial presentation' objects='specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic or outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention services' id='activity14'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='medical attention is sought within 48 hours after symptom onset' id='branch41'>
              <activity role='ED physician' action='confirm the immediate location of care based on symptom progression and access to specialized services' objects='presentation timing, symptom progression, and specialized clinic access assessment' id='activity14_1'/>
              <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway15'>
                <branch condition='fluctuating or crescendo hemibody motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech difficulty is present, or delay to a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic poses risk to patient health' id='branch17'>
                  <activity role='ED physician' action='evaluate patient in an appropriately resourced ED' objects='high-risk TIA or minor stroke patient' id='activity15'/>
                  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway6'>
                    <branch condition='inpatient evaluation and management for stroke prevention is indicated' id='branch19'>
                      <activity role='ED physician' action='admit to hospital for inpatient evaluation and management for stroke prevention' objects='hospital admission' id='activity16'/>
                    </branch>
                    <branch condition='inpatient evaluation and management for stroke prevention is not indicated' id='branch20'>
                      <activity role='ED physician' action='continue urgent ambulatory stroke-prevention evaluation' objects='ambulatory stroke-prevention plan' id='activity17'/>
                    </branch>
                  </exclusiveGateway>
                </branch>
                <branch condition='no fluctuating or crescendo hemibody motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech difficulty is present and a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic is accessible without harmful delay' id='branch18'>
                  <activity role='ED physician' action='arrange immediate care at a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic' objects='specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic referral' id='activity18'/>
                </branch>
              </exclusiveGateway>
            </branch>
          </exclusiveGateway>
          <activity role='stroke care team' action='perform urgent evaluation including brain CT scan as soon as possible, and for patients presenting within 48 hours no later than 24 hours after symptom onset' objects='brain CT scan and urgent stroke evaluation' id='activity19'/>
          <activity role='stroke care team' action='review brain CT result for intracranial hemorrhage' objects='brain CT result' id='activity20_1'/>
          <activity role='stroke care team' action='review current antiplatelet use' objects='medication history' id='activity20_2'/>
          <activity role='stroke care team' action='assess contraindications to antiplatelet therapy' objects='contraindications' id='activity20_3'/>
          <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway7'>
            <branch condition='antiplatelet therapy is contraindicated' id='branch21'>
              <activity role='stroke care team' action='withhold antiplatelet initiation and document contraindication' objects='contraindication record' id='activity21'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='patient is already on an antiplatelet agent and antiplatelet therapy is not contraindicated' id='branch22'>
              <activity role='stroke care team' action='continue current antiplatelet therapy and tailor other stroke-prevention treatments' objects='current antiplatelet regimen and stroke-prevention treatments' id='activity22'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='patient is not on an antiplatelet agent and antiplatelet therapy is not contraindicated' id='branch23'>
              <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway8'>
                <branch condition='ASA regimen is selected' id='branch24'>
                  <activity role='ED nurse' action='perform swallowing screening before ASA administration' objects='swallowing screen' id='activity23'/>
                  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway9'>
                    <branch condition='patient fails swallowing screening' id='branch27'>
                      <activity role='stroke care team' action='offer rectal or gastric ASA 160 mg loading dose as necessary, followed by daily ASA when feasible' objects='rectal or gastric ASA and ASA maintenance regimen' id='activity24'/>
                    </branch>
                    <branch condition='patient passes swallowing screening' id='branch28'>
                      <activity role='stroke care team' action='start ASA 160 mg loading dose followed by enteric coated ASA 81 to 325 mg daily, usually 81 mg/day maintenance' objects='ASA regimen' id='activity25'/>
                    </branch>
                  </exclusiveGateway>
                </branch>
                <branch condition='clopidogrel regimen is selected' id='branch25'>
                  <activity role='stroke care team' action='start clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose followed by 75 mg/day' objects='clopidogrel regimen' id='activity26'/>
                </branch>
                <branch condition='extended-release dipyridamole plus ASA regimen is selected' id='branch26'>
                  <activity role='ED nurse' action='perform swallowing screening before ASA loading dose' objects='swallowing screen' id='activity27'/>
                  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway10'>
                    <branch condition='patient fails swallowing screening' id='branch29'>
                      <activity role='stroke care team' action='offer rectal or gastric ASA 160 mg loading dose as necessary and then start extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg plus ASA 25 mg twice daily when feasible' objects='rectal or gastric ASA and extended-release dipyridamole/ASA regimen' id='activity28'/>
                    </branch>
                    <branch condition='patient passes swallowing screening' id='branch30'>
                      <activity role='stroke care team' action='load with ASA 160 mg first and then start extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg plus ASA 25 mg twice daily' objects='extended-release dipyridamole/ASA regimen' id='activity29'/>
                    </branch>
                  </exclusiveGateway>
                </branch>
              </exclusiveGateway>
            </branch>
          </exclusiveGateway>
          <activity role='stroke care team' action='initiate other stroke-prevention treatments tailored to each patient' objects='tailored stroke-prevention treatments' id='activity30'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='TIA without high-risk features' id='branch15'>
          <activity role='ED team' action='perform brain CT scan as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after initial presentation' objects='brain CT scan' id='activity31'/>
          <activity role='ED team' action='review brain CT result for intracranial hemorrhage' objects='brain CT result' id='activity32_1'/>
          <activity role='ED team' action='review current antiplatelet use' objects='medication history' id='activity32_2'/>
          <activity role='ED team' action='assess contraindications to antiplatelet therapy' objects='contraindications' id='activity32_3'/>
          <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway11'>
            <branch condition='antiplatelet therapy is contraindicated' id='branch31'>
              <activity role='ED physician' action='withhold antiplatelet initiation and document contraindication' objects='contraindication record' id='activity33'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='patient is already on an antiplatelet agent and antiplatelet therapy is not contraindicated' id='branch32'>
              <activity role='ED physician' action='continue current antiplatelet therapy and tailor other stroke-prevention treatments' objects='current antiplatelet regimen and stroke-prevention treatments' id='activity34'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='patient is not on an antiplatelet agent and antiplatelet therapy is not contraindicated' id='branch33'>
              <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway12'>
                <branch condition='ASA regimen is selected' id='branch34'>
                  <activity role='ED nurse' action='perform swallowing screening before ASA administration' objects='swallowing screen' id='activity35'/>
                  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway13'>
                    <branch condition='patient fails swallowing screening' id='branch37'>
                      <activity role='ED physician' action='offer rectal or gastric ASA 160 mg loading dose as necessary, followed by daily ASA when feasible' objects='rectal or gastric ASA and ASA maintenance regimen' id='activity36'/>
                    </branch>
                    <branch condition='patient passes swallowing screening' id='branch38'>
                      <activity role='ED physician' action='start ASA 160 mg loading dose followed by enteric coated ASA 81 to 325 mg daily, usually 81 mg/day maintenance' objects='ASA regimen' id='activity37'/>
                    </branch>
                  </exclusiveGateway>
                </branch>
                <branch condition='clopidogrel regimen is selected' id='branch35'>
                  <activity role='ED physician' action='start clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose followed by 75 mg/day' objects='clopidogrel regimen' id='activity38'/>
                </branch>
                <branch condition='extended-release dipyridamole plus ASA regimen is selected' id='branch36'>
                  <activity role='ED nurse' action='perform swallowing screening before ASA loading dose' objects='swallowing screen' id='activity39'/>
                  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway14'>
                    <branch condition='patient fails swallowing screening' id='branch39'>
                      <activity role='ED physician' action='offer rectal or gastric ASA 160 mg loading dose as necessary and then start extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg plus ASA 25 mg twice daily when feasible' objects='rectal or gastric ASA and extended-release dipyridamole/ASA regimen' id='activity40'/>
                    </branch>
                    <branch condition='patient passes swallowing screening' id='branch40'>
                      <activity role='ED physician' action='load with ASA 160 mg first and then start extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg plus ASA 25 mg twice daily' objects='extended-release dipyridamole/ASA regimen' id='activity41'/>
                    </branch>
                  </exclusiveGateway>
                </branch>
              </exclusiveGateway>
            </branch>
          </exclusiveGateway>
          <activity role='ED physician' action='refer patient to an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention services for comprehensive evaluation and management within 1 month of symptom onset' objects='outpatient stroke-prevention clinic referral' id='activity42'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
  </parallelGateway>
  <activity role='care team' action='assess cross-continuum secondary prevention needs' objects='Modules 5 and 10 secondary prevention requirements' id='activity43_1'/>
  <activity role='care team' action='provide or prescribe appropriate secondary prevention therapies' objects='secondary prevention therapies' id='activity43_2'/>
  <activity role='care team' action='arrange follow-up or referral for ongoing prevention management whether the patient is admitted or discharged' objects='cross-continuum prevention follow-up plan' id='activity43_3'/>
</process>
suggestion:
Problem: In the high-risk/minor stroke pathway, the branch with id='branch16' under the exclusive gateway with id='exclusivegateway5' is logically incorrect because activity id='activity14' (“refer for evaluation within 24 hours of initial presentation”) is followed by the common activities id='activity19' to id='activity30', which means patients who seek medical attention more than 48 hours after symptom onset are still forced into the same immediate urgent CT/antiplatelet evaluation block as the within-48-hour patients.

Suggested modification: Adjust the scope and order of the branches under id='exclusivegateway5'.
- Keep id='activity14' inside id='branch16' as the main action for the “more than 48 hours after symptom onset” branch.
- Remove the common placement of id='activity19', id='activity20_1', id='activity20_2', id='activity20_3', id='exclusivegateway7', and id='activity30' after id='exclusivegateway5'.
- Place the urgent CT and antiplatelet initiation sequence only in the branch for patients presenting within 48 hours after symptom onset, or, if you want to model the >48-hour pathway in detail, create a separate evaluation sequence inside id='branch16' with the referred service as the role and timing changed to “within 24 hours of initial presentation,” not “no later than 24 hours after symptom onset.”

Reason: The recommendation clearly separates these two situations. For patients presenting within 48 hours, urgent CT and antiplatelet treatment should occur as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset. For patients presenting more than 48 hours after symptom onset, the guideline says they should be referred for evaluation within 24 hours of initial presentation. Therefore, the current design makes the exclusive gateway ineffective and mixes two different clinical timing rules into one downstream sequence.
You need to modify the process text according to the suggestions and output it.
Be careful not to change the format:

When the process starts, please output '<process>'
An activity is represented as follows:
<activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
The exclusive gateway is represented as follows:
<exclusiveGateway id=''>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</exclusiveGateway>
The parallel gateway is represented as follows:
<parallelGateway id=''>
  <branch id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</parallelGateway>
The inclusive gateway is represented as follows:
<inclusiveGateway id=''>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</inclusiveGateway>
At the end of the process, please output '</process>'

There are a few requirements that should be noted when generating process text:
    1) role, action, objects and id in an activity represent the subject of the activity, the activity behavior, the items needed to perform in the activity and the activity id, respectively.
    2) There are at least two branches within the exclusive gateway and the parallel gateway.
    3) Activity, gateway, and branch id attributes must be unique.
    4) When the branch in the exclusive gateway has a condition, fill the condition into the condition attribute. When the branch unconditional within the exclusive gateway, the condition attribute of the branch =''. However, all branches within a exclusive gateway can only be conditional, or both unconditional.
    5) The branch in the parallel gateway has no condition attribute.
    6) Each branch in the inclusive gateway must have condition.
    7) There must be at least one activity inside the branch tag.
    8) The activity of a sequential relation need not be represented by a gateway, that is, there is no 'sequenceGateway' label.

[2026-07-04 22:57:49 INFO] Process design reviewer: **Process design reviewer<->Team leader on : GiveSuggestion, turn 0**

[You're a team leader. Now, we share a common interest in collaborating to successfully complete a task assigned by a new customer.
Your main responsibilities will include guiding the process design expert through process generation, process refinement, and so on.
Here is a new customer's task: Module 3: Admission to Acute Care
This module identifies best practices for acute inpatient admissions (as described in recommendation
2.4.2 for the specific group of TIA patients who should be admitted). Module 3a provides
recommendations for acute inpatient treatment, and module 3b provides recommendations for the
prevention of secondary complications, which should be initiated in the acute care setting but also be
considered as ongoing activities. All patients should be given appropriate cross-continuum
prevention assessment and therapies (Modules 5 and 10). The Ontario Stroke Network revised the
definition of a stroke unit “A geographical unit with identifiable co-located beds (eg 5A -7, 5A-8,
5A-9, 5A-10, 5A-11) that are occupied by stroke patients 75% of the time and has a dedicated
interprofessional team with expertise in stroke care with the following professionals at a minimum
nursing, physiotherapy, occupational therapy, speech language pathologist”.) Ontario Stroke
Network 2015
Module 3a: Acute Care Treatment
Module 3a Recommended Practices 
3.1 Stroke units
3.1.1 Patients should be admitted to a specialized, geographically
defined hospital unit dedicated to the management of stroke patients
A geographical unit with identifiable co-located beds (eg 5A -7, 5A-
8, 5A-9, 5A-10, 5A-11) that are occupied by stroke patients 75% of
the time and has a dedicated inter-professional team with expertise in
stroke care with the following professionals at a minimum nursing,
physiotherapy, occupational therapy, speech language pathologist”.
• This recommendation is in accordance with the HQO Effectiveness
of Stroke Unit Care: A Special Report, which concluded (61):
Moderate quality evidence showed that persons admitted to a stroke
unit had a significant reduction in death and the combined outcome
of death or institutionalization, and a nonsignificant reduction in
institutionalization. Low quality evidence showed that patients
admitted to a stroke unit had a significant reduction in the combined
outcome of death or dependency and length of hospital stay and a
nonsignificant reduction in the outcome of dependency
3.1.2 The core stroke unit team should consist of health care
professionals with stroke expertise in medicine, nursing, occupational
therapy, physiotherapy, speech-language pathology, social work, and
clinical nutrition (a dietitian)
3.1.3 To have the necessary stroke expertise, the health care
professionals on the core stroke unit team should be individuals who
spend the vast majority of their time treating stroke patients and
regularly complete education about stroke care
3.1.4 Patients should have access to a specialized interprofessional
team 7 days a week
3.1.5 Physiotherapy, speech-language pathology, and occupational
therapy team members should assess stroke patients within 48 hours
of admission to hospital and formulate a management plan
3.2 Early mobilization
3.2.1 All stroke patients admitted to hospital with acute stroke should
be mobilized early(between 24 hours and 48 hours of stroke onset),
unless contraindicated
Frequent out-of-bed activity in the very early time frame (within 24
hours of stroke onset) is not recommended Mobilization may be
reasonable for some patients with acute stroke in the very early time
frame and clinical judgement should be used.
3.3 Assessment
3.3.1 Clinicians should use standardized, valid assessment tools to
evaluate patients’ stroke-related impairments and functional status
3.3.2 AlphaFIM should be completed on or by day 3 after
admission(target day 3, admission day is day 1)
3.3.3 All stroke patients should be assessed for risk of developing
venous thromboembolism
3.3.4 Early mobilization and adequate hydration should be encouraged
for all acute stroke patients to help prevent venous thromboembolism
3.3.5 The nutrition and hydration status of stroke patients should be
assessed within the first 48 hours of admission using a valid screening
tool. Stroke patients with nutritional concerns, hydration deficits,
dysphagia, or other comorbidities should be referred to a dietitian.
Referral to a dietitian should be made within 7 days of admission for
recommendations and for consideration of enteral nutrition support for
patients who are unable to meet nutritional and fluid requirements
3.4 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
3.5 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies
3.5.1 All patients, whether admitted to in-hospital rehabilitation or
discharged, should be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary
prevention assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10)
Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; Australia, Australian Clinical Guidelines for Stroke
Management; CSBPR, Canadian Best Practices Recommendations; FIM, Functional Independence Measure; HQO, Health Quality Ontario;
OSN, Ontario Stroke Network; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack.

The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel.

Module 3a Implementation Considerations
General considerations
• All hospitals providing stroke/TIA care should ensure that the interprofessional stroke team uses standardized, validated
assessment tools; where possible, these tools should be in electronic format linked to the EHR
• Patients should be screened/assessed for diabetes, dental issues, cognitive impairment, and depression upon
presentation to the ED, and the results should be forwarded to the TIA clinic for follow-up
• Hospitals should ensure adequate staffing 7 days per week
Abbreviations: ED, emergency department; EHR, electronic health record; TIA, transient ischemic attack..
To complete the task, I will give you one or more instructions, and you must help me to write a specific solution that appropriately solves the requested instruction based on your expertise and my needs.]

Problem: The exclusive gateway with id='eg2' is logically incorrect because the branches `br9` and `br10` are modeled as mutually exclusive alternatives, but for the same patient they can occur sequentially over time.

Suggested modification: Replace the single exclusive gateway `eg2` with two decision steps:
1. First use an exclusive gateway to decide whether mobilization is contraindicated.
   - If contraindicated, perform `act15`.
   - If not contraindicated, continue to a second decision.
2. Then use a second exclusive gateway to decide the current time window since stroke onset.
   - If `< 24 hours`, perform `act13`, then add a follow-up activity such as “reassess mobilization readiness at 24 hours after stroke onset”, and after reassessment perform `act14` when the patient reaches the `24 to 48 hours` window and still has no contraindication.
   - If `24 to 48 hours`, perform `act14` directly.

Reason: For a patient admitted early after stroke onset, the process should first limit frequent out-of-bed activity within the first 24 hours and then later start early mobilization between 24 and 48 hours if appropriate. The current exclusive gateway wrongly forces the process to choose only one of these time-based paths, which does not reflect the clinical sequence described in Module 3a.

   - If not contraindicated, continue to a second decision.

   - If `< 24 hours`, perform `act13`, then add a follow-up activity such as “reassess mobilization readiness at 24 hours after stroke onset”, and after reassessment perform `act14` when the patient reaches the `24 to 48 hours` window and still has no contraindication.
   - If `24 to 48 hours`, perform `act14` directly.

First use an exclusive gateway to decide whether mobilization is contraindicated.\n   - If contraindicated, perform act15.\n   - If not contraindicated, continue to a second decision.\n2. Then use a second exclusive gateway to decide the current time window since stroke onset.\n   - If &lt; 24 hours, perform act13, then add a follow-up activity such as “reassess mobilization readiness at 24 hours after stroke onset”, and after reassessment perform act14 when the patient reaches the 24 to 48 hours window and still has no contraindication.\n   - If 24 to 48 hours, perform act14 directly.\n\nReason: For a patient admitted early after stroke onset, the process should first limit frequent out-of-bed activity within the first 24 hours and then later start early mobilization between 24 and 48 hours if appropriate. | **task_prompt** | Module 3: Admission to Acute Care This module identifies best practices for acute inpatient admissions (as described in recommendation 2.4.2 for the specific group of TIA patients who should be admitted). Module 3a provides recommendations for acute inpatient treatment, and module 3b provides recommendations for the prevention of secondary complications, which should be initiated in the acute care setting but also be considered as ongoing activities. All patients should be given appropriate cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies (Modules 5 and 10). The Ontario Stroke Network revised the definition of a stroke unit “A geographical unit with identifiable co-located beds (eg 5A -7, 5A-8, 5A-9, 5A-10, 5A-11) that are occupied by stroke patients 75% of the time and has a dedicated interprofessional team with expertise in stroke care with the following professionals at a minimum nursing, physiotherapy, occupational therapy, speech language pathologist”.) Ontario Stroke Network 2015 Module 3a: Acute Care Treatment Module 3a Recommended Practices  3.1 Stroke units 3.1.1 Patients should be admitted to a specialized, geographically defined hospital unit dedicated to the management of stroke patients A geographical unit with identifiable co-located beds (eg 5A -7, 5A- 8, 5A-9, 5A-10, 5A-11) that are occupied by stroke patients 75% of the time and has a dedicated inter-professional team with expertise in stroke care with the following professionals at a minimum nursing, physiotherapy, occupational therapy, speech language pathologist”. • This recommendation is in accordance with the HQO Effectiveness of Stroke Unit Care: A Special Report, which concluded (61): Moderate quality evidence showed that persons admitted to a stroke unit had a significant reduction in death and the combined outcome of death or institutionalization, and a nonsignificant reduction in institutionalization. Low quality evidence showed that patients admitted to a stroke unit had a significant reduction in the combined outcome of death or dependency and length of hospital stay and a nonsignificant reduction in the outcome of dependency 3.1.2 The core stroke unit team should consist of health care professionals with stroke expertise in medicine, nursing, occupational therapy, physiotherapy, speech-language pathology, social work, and clinical nutrition (a dietitian) 3.1.3 To have the necessary stroke expertise, the health care professionals on the core stroke unit team should be individuals who spend the vast majority of their time treating stroke patients and regularly complete education about stroke care 3.1.4 Patients should have access to a specialized interprofessional team 7 days a week 3.1.5 Physiotherapy, speech-language pathology, and occupational therapy team members should assess stroke patients within 48 hours of admission to hospital and formulate a management plan 3.2 Early mobilization 3.2.1 All stroke patients admitted to hospital with acute stroke should be mobilized early(between 24 hours and 48 hours of stroke onset), unless contraindicated Frequent out-of-bed activity in the very early time frame (within 24 hours of stroke onset) is not recommended Mobilization may be reasonable for some patients with acute stroke in the very early time frame and clinical judgement should be used. 3.3 Assessment 3.3.1 Clinicians should use standardized, valid assessment tools to evaluate patients’ stroke-related impairments and functional status 3.3.2 AlphaFIM should be completed on or by day 3 after admission(target day 3, admission day is day 1) 3.3.3 All stroke patients should be assessed for risk of developing venous thromboembolism 3.3.4 Early mobilization and adequate hydration should be encouraged for all acute stroke patients to help prevent venous thromboembolism 3.3.5 The nutrition and hydration status of stroke patients should be assessed within the first 48 hours of admission using a valid screening tool. Stroke patients with nutritional concerns, hydration deficits, dysphagia, or other comorbidities should be referred to a dietitian. Referral to a dietitian should be made within 7 days of admission for recommendations and for consideration of enteral nutrition support for patients who are unable to meet nutritional and fluid requirements 3.4 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke 3.5 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies 3.5.1 All patients, whether admitted to in-hospital rehabilitation or discharged, should be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary prevention assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10) Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; Australia, Australian Clinical Guidelines for Stroke Management; CSBPR, Canadian Best Practices Recommendations; FIM, Functional Independence Measure; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; OSN, Ontario Stroke Network; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack. The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel. Module 3a Implementation Considerations General considerations • All hospitals providing stroke/TIA care should ensure that the interprofessional stroke team uses standardized, validated assessment tools; where possible, these tools should be in electronic format linked to the EHR • Patients should be screened/assessed for diabetes, dental issues, cognitive impairment, and depression upon presentation to the ED, and the results should be forwarded to the TIA clinic for follow-up • Hospitals should ensure adequate staffing 7 days per week Abbreviations: ED, emergency department; EHR, electronic health record; TIA, transient ischemic attack. |
